food_subgroup: Spiced tomato-cream chicken curry with steamed rice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Butter Chicken (Murgh Makhani). The nearest parent concept is a spiced chicken curry dish. A new FoodOn concept is recommended for this dish.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat-based curry dishes — chicken in spiced tomato cream sauce (closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Butter Chicken / Murgh Makhani)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Butter Chicken. A03XB used as closest defensible parent for meat-based curry dishes.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for Butter Chicken / Murgh Makhani as of April 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of butter chicken with rice is approximately 450 g, comprising roughly 200–220 g of makhani curry and 230–250 g of steamed basmati rice.

nutrient_source.title: Butter Chicken — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, Food Code F11020052
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), Hyderabad
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN food code F11020052 (Butter Chicken) used as the primary nutrient anchor. The record represents butter chicken served with steamed basmati rice; the composite per-100 g values reflect the mixed dish as consumed. Micronutrients copper, manganese, and vitamin K were supplemented from enriched-JSON estimates (confidence 0.9) where ICMR-NIN did not report these values. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are not reported by ICMR-NIN for this preparation and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — white rice (jasmine/basmati) as primary GI contributor; butter chicken sauce component estimated from mixed-dish analogy
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 58 (medium) is anchored to white jasmine/basmati rice as the dominant carbohydrate contributor (GI ~58–64 per University of Sydney GI Database). The butter chicken sauce component contains cream, butter, and protein which slow gastric emptying and lower the composite GI. Glycemic load of 40.6 calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 15.5 × 4.5) / 100 ≈ 40.5, rounded to 40.6 for a 450 g serving. This is a high GL per serving, reflecting the large portion size.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — approximately 1701 mg per 450 g serving (74% of the 2300 mg daily recommended limit)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200–250 g and request reduced-salt preparation, as a standard restaurant serving provides approximately three-quarters of the daily sodium allowance from butter, cream, and added salt in the makhani sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (58) with high glycemic load per serving (GL ~40) due to the large rice component; fat and protein in the sauce moderately blunt postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the rice portion to 100–150 g and increase the curry proportion to lower the glycemic load per meal, as the high fat and protein content of the makhani sauce slows gastric emptying but the total carbohydrate load from a full 450 g serving remains substantial.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content of 2.8 g per 100 g (12.6 g per 450 g serving) from butter and heavy cream, combined with cholesterol at 48 mg per 100 g (216 mg per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in reduced portions, as the saturated fat from butter and cream contributes significantly to daily saturated fat limits; requesting a lighter cream preparation or substituting yogurt for cream can reduce the saturated fat load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, garam masala) partially offset pro-inflammatory saturated fat from butter and cream
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Butter chicken contains turmeric and garam masala which provide curcumin with anti-inflammatory properties; however, the saturated fat from butter and cream may promote inflammation, so moderate consumption is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Butter Chicken with Rice (Murgh Makhani) is one of India's most iconic and globally recognised dishes, originating at the Moti Mahal restaurant in Delhi in the 1950s. The dish consists of tandoor-roasted chicken pieces simmered in a velvety, mildly spiced makhani sauce made from slow-cooked puréed tomatoes, generous amounts of butter (makhan), heavy cream, cashew paste, ginger, garlic, and aromatic spices including garam masala and kasuri methi (dried fenugreek leaves). The sauce has a characteristically rich, orange-red colour with a mild, creamy sweetness balanced by warm spices. It is served over steamed basmati rice, making it a complete mixed dish. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 143 kcal, 8.8 g protein, 5.8 g fat (2.8 g saturated), and 15.5 g carbohydrate. A standard restaurant serving of 450 g delivers around 644 kcal and 1701 mg sodium, making sodium management important for individuals with hypertension. The medium GI of approximately 58 is driven primarily by the rice component, while the fat and protein in the sauce moderate the postprandial glycaemic response. The dish is halal and gluten-free. It is widely available at Indian restaurants in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, and is commonly ordered as a lunch or dinner main course, either as a set meal or takeaway.

can a diabetic eat butter chicken
butter chicken vs tikka masala difference
how much sodium in butter chicken

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and most micronutrients sourced from ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition), the primary approved source for Indian dishes. Three micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from enriched-JSON estimates at confidence 0.9 where ICMR-NIN did not report values. GI anchored to University of Sydney GI Database via analogous-estimate method for white rice as the dominant carbohydrate contributor.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this butter chicken with rice preparation not reported by ICMR-NIN (food code F11020052) as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported by ICMR-NIN for this composite dish. Copper (0.1 mg/100 g), manganese (0.05 mg/100 g), and vitamin K (1.5 mcg/100 g) sourced from enriched-JSON estimates at confidence 0.9 as ICMR-NIN did not report these micronutrients for this food code. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate anchored to white jasmine/basmati rice (University of Sydney GI Database); the composite dish GI is moderated by the high fat and protein content of the makhani sauce. Portions array from input contained several duplicates and one erroneous label ('1 medium pizza') which have been cleaned; the canonical serving size of 450 g (1 plate curry + rice) is retained as default. The input source URL (https://www.nin.res.in/) is the ICMR-NIN homepage; no direct deep-link to the specific food code page is publicly available.
